title: Octopus color vision
question: Octopuses are monochromats yet achieve precise color-matched camouflage. The off-axis blur hypothesis (Stubbs & Stubbs 2016) is contested for broadband natural surfaces. Skin photoreception via the same opsin cascade has been demonstrated in excised tissue but not confirmed to function in live camouflage. How they do it remains genuinely open.

title: Tip-of-the-tongue: why stopping helps
question: The tip-of-the-tongue state reliably resolves when you stop trying, but two accounts — interference-clearing (actively generated wrong candidates block access) and unconscious processing (retrieval continues subthreshold) — make identical predictions and have not been separated experimentally.

title: Why insight coherence misreports accuracy
question: At the moment of insight, a dopaminergic signal fires on coherence, not on verified correctness. Insight solutions are more accurate than analytical ones on average (57% vs. 37%), but the confidence signal is less predictive of correctness than analytical confidence. What determines when coherence tracks truth and when it doesn't is unknown.

title: Hollow face illusion: where the prior runs
question: The face-convexity prior overrides correct binocular depth information even when the observer knows exactly what they are seeing. Schizophrenia patients and cannabis users are less susceptible — reduced top-down signaling lets the correct depth signal win. What neural circuit implements this prior, and why explicit knowledge cannot access it to override it, is not known.

title: McCollough effect: 85-day persistence
question: Color aftereffects induced by viewing colored gratings can persist for up to 85 days — orders of magnitude longer than any other adaptation effect. No decay model accounts for this duration. The mechanism of storage and the reason for this specific timescale remain unknown after six decades of investigation.

title: Fingerprint ridge orientation: what breaks symmetry
question: Fingerprint ridge patterns are produced by reaction-diffusion dynamics — the EDAR-WNT-BMP pathway determines that ridges will form, but the specific orientation and uniqueness of each print emerges from geometry and chance fluctuations in initial conditions. What event or gradient fixes the initial symmetry-breaking point, and why it is irreproducible even in identical twins, remains unresolved.

title: Prion spontaneous conversion
question: Sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease — the most common prion disease — has no known exposure and no identified genetic cause. PrPC converts to PrPSc without an external template. The mechanism of this spontaneous conversion, and what determines why it happens to specific individuals, is not understood. The atomic structure of infectious PrPSc also remains unresolved by cryo-EM and NMR.

title: Which memories survive metamorphosis
question: Memories encoded late in larval development (fifth instar) survive metamorphosis into adult moths; memories encoded early (third instar) do not. The difference correlates with which mushroom body neurons are present at training time and which survive histolysis. But what exactly preserves late-born neurons' connectivity through the histolysis process — while early neurons are pruned — remains unknown.

title: How desert ants recalibrate step count
question: Cataglyphis ants raised from birth on stilts calibrate their step counter correctly to the altered stride length — the developmental window is real and functional. But there is no identified receptor for absolute leg length, and no known sensory pathway that could provide the information needed for this calibration. How the nervous system learns the correct conversion factor is not known.

title: Scrub jay episodic memory: what 'like' is protecting
question: Scrub jays demonstrate what-where-when memory, privacy-sensitive cache behavior, and modeling of another bird's specific knowledge of a past event. The qualifier 'episodic-like' preserves the criterion of autonoetic consciousness — the sense of mental time travel. Whether that criterion can be tested behaviorally is unclear; it has never been verified behaviorally even in humans. The question may not be reachable by available methods.

title: What crosses the boundary in sensory substitution
question: After training with tactile vision substitution, blind subjects stop reporting sensations on their skin and begin reporting objects localized in external space. Active movement is necessary for this shift, and learning transfers across hands and orientations. But what changes in the nervous system when the experience migrates from proximal to distal — and why active movement is the trigger — is not understood.

title: Avian magnetoreception: quantum to neural
question: European robins use an inclination compass that is disrupted by radiofrequency noise at field strengths too weak to affect classical chemistry — consistent with a radical pair mechanism in CRY4. But the coupling between the quantum spin state and downstream neural signaling is not known. How the bird converts a molecular spin-correlation measurement into a navigational signal remains unresolved.

title: Why the chronic pain prior doesn't update
question: In predictive processing, chronic pain appears to be a self-sustaining prior: the brain predicts pain, the experience confirms it, and the signal from the periphery cannot break in as a correction. But prediction error signals must still be arriving — they are not simply absent. Why those signals fail to update the prior, when normally mismatched predictions trigger revision, is not understood.

title: Yeast prion transmission: why only some cells
question: The [PSI+] yeast prion is propagated faithfully through cell division, but only some cells that contain Sup35 protein spontaneously convert to the prion state. The conditions that trigger initial conversion in susceptible cells, and why most cells remain in the non-prion state despite containing the same protein, are not understood.
